data.title: Strawberry Fields Not Getting Picked, As This REIT's Debt Load Could Be A Jam
data.description:
Today's Stock: A Healthcare REIT Blooming Across America's Heartland

No, it does not own strawberry farms, but rather a portfolio of healthcare and skilled nursing facilities across the US.

Strawberry Fields REIT (STRW) is the focus of today's article, and according to its Seeking Alpha profile, "the company’s portfolio includes 142 healthcare facilities with an aggregate of 15,500+ beds."

In the wake of its earnings beat on Nov. 6th, we'll take a closer look at where this REIT may be heading, and explore its potential as a dividend idea as well. This is my initial coverage of this stock.

My Rating: Hold

For my initial coverage of this stock, I'm going neutral.

I'll be arguing that although the biggest upside may come from macroeconomic demand for skilled nursing/seniorcare facilities, and this company is on a portfolio growth spree lately, several other factors weigh down on this firm particularly a heavy leverage compared to peers, and lack of long-term dividend growth, as well as trading at a large premium to book value compared to upside forecasts.

Here is a worksheet showing how I came to this conclusion and what drove it:

Long-Term Return vs S&P500: A Positive Performance, But Far Below the Index

The first rating category I considered was how this stock performed over 10 years in comparison to a major benchmark like the S&P500 index, and how a few peers did too:

In this comparison, TRW saw a 10 year price return of nearly +30%, which is positive however far below the return achieved by the benchmark for that same period, so if I were to invest in an S&P500 index fund it may have performed better than investing in this stock. At the same time, though, STRW did better than two REIT peers shown, both of whom have exposure to the same property niche such as senior-care, etc.

For this reason, I am neutral in this category, as the stock has shown positive momentum over a longer period, but not enough to meet or beat a major index. Grant it, I realize that the index has a lot of major tech names, and the AI revolution has fueled massive bullishness there, but nevertheless the gap in the chart above is quite wide.

Sector Risk: Indicators Of Continued Demand Outpacing Supply, Some Say

Moving along, we should consider the sector risk and sector opportunity when it comes to healthcare REITs, which I think is is a very different niche than other REITS and should not be lumped in with comparing against residential, retail, or farmland REITs, for instance.

To get a sense of this niche within REITS, here is an example of one of the properties owned by STRW, according to their portfolio, a 181-bed facility that includes amenities like rehab, skilled nursing, and activities.

As that sets the stage for what macro factors to look for, I found some forecasts pointing to continued upside potential for skilled nursing.

For instance, it was reported in a major financial site that research firm Research and Markets projects a "CAGR of 4.4%" by 2033 for this niche:

Further data shows that an aging population will continue driving demand for senior living properties, and I think this can be an upside opportunity for this niche.

For instance, back in September, CNBC reported:

There will be just about 4,000 new senior living units developed this year and next year, but demand growth would necessitate 100,000 new beds each year through 2040, according to data from the National Investment Center for Seniors Housing and Care.

I think this is favorable data for a REIT specializing in this niche, if we are trying to guess what the music will do several years from now, or whether it will stop, or just slow down.

In fact, the big Wall St players are already jumping on this opportunity. Just days ago, real estate industry portal The Real Deal reported that "banking giant Morgan Stanley showed bullishness on Denver-area senior housing, dropping $305 million on portfolio transactions recently."

We can also see from Seeking Alpha that ratings are predominantly bullish or neutral-to-bullish on this peer group of healthcare REITs I selected, with exposure to senior living, which I think shows a lot of market optimism in this niche, despite some of these stocks having vastly underperformed the S&P500 index (a tech-heavy index).

So, given the factors above, particularly what I expect to be a supply/demand imbalance, I am highly bullish in this category of sector risk.

Company Growth: A REIT on An Acquisition Spree, Spreading Across Middle America

Going beyond just macreconomic trends, when it comes to thinking about company-specific growth at a REIT, as in prior articles, and in my book I wrote on REITs, usually I look for lease growth, stable and high levels of occupancy, geographic diversification, portfolio growth, and growth via acquisitions.

Here is what I learned from the firm's Q3 presentation released in early November.

We can see, for instance, that this REIT has been on a shopping spree this year, as the investor deck (pg 12) shows, with 5 property acquisitions which I think could help drive future revenue growth:

We can also see this REIT has achieved a great deal of geographic diversity, as the geomap below shows, which I think reduces the downside risk of over-concentration in a single state or single healthcare operator:

As for occupancy levels, let's say a good benchmark is 75% or better. Well, the investor deck mentions "SNF facility occupancy of 74.2%," so it is pretty close to my benchmark.

If not knowing that this was a REIT, at first glance towards the company logo I would have thought they sell strawberry jam, or own strawberry farms, both of which I love, but looking past the logo this firm has proven itself in achieving an impressive CAGR in base rent, which I think is more indication of demand. Consider the chart below from the investor deck (pg. 21):

While that rent CAGR is mostly based on historic data, a forward-looking metric I would mention is that expected fwd revenue growth for STRW is +18.4%, which puts it in the middle of this peer group, as LTC and CTRE are leading the pack in that metric.

Thus, considering the portfolio growth, rent growth, moderate-to-high fwd revenue growth expected, and geo diversity, I am compelled to be highly bullish in this category. Of the factors mentioned, I think the most weight should go to portfolio growth, as it can help this REIT meet the market demand for these properties, while residents or potential ones can have additional options. For instance, a resident from Indiana may choose a facility in Texas if it means being near children and grandchildren who can visit them often.

Profit Margins: Beating Key Peers In Same Niche, While Growing AFFO Steadily

Next, let's consider the topic of profit margins.

When it comes to a REIT, the metrics I focus on is the EBITDA margin primarily, FFO growth, and net income margin to a lesser extent because it can be impacted by significant property depreciation (a non-cash, non-operating expense). Buildings for commercial/business use can depreciate, after all.

In the table above, for instance, we can see the wide gap between this firm's EBITDA margin and its net margin. At the same time, comparing against this peer group, with an EBITDA margin of +84% STRW leads the group, and indicating strong core business operating efficiency.

Since AFFO is a key metric that could indicate whether the firm can cover its dividend, I would point out from the investor deck (pg 20) that this REIT has achieved a proven AFFO growth steadily since 2020, and has a positive guidance for full-year FY2025, as the chart below shows, but also a positive outlook this year on EBITDA:

In his Q3 commentary, Chairman & CEO Moishe Gubin set a positive tone, saying "as we near year-end, I anticipate the Company being able to maintain this momentum and close the year strong."

However, the REIT sector often has to rely on debt financing (loans, bonds, etc) so interest expense is a thing to keep an eye on, on the cost side. For instance, the Q3 press release mentioned that "interest expense increased by $4.4MM or 51% primarily due to additional interest incurred from a new commercial bank loan, the issuance of Series A and Series B Bond issuances."

Considering that interest expense risk, but also the positive factors mentioned in this section, and they are several, I would go with a cautious bullish score in this category.

Dividend Growth & Coverage: An Elevated Payout Ratio Could Pose Downside Risk

Now, I've often written on this site that dividend income can be one appeal for many investors who buy REIT stocks. Personally, I've been growing a diversified REIT portfolio (although I don't currently own this stock) and I have a mix of those who pay monthly and quarterly dividends. One such healthcare stock I've been picking up a few shares in during dips has been Healthpeak Properties (DOC), to wet my beak in the healthcare niche.

That one was not a comparison used, since it is not so focused on senior living/assisted care.

With that said, what I think is common to all REITS is that many investors care about higher yields than some other sectors, while other investors may care more about dividend growth.

For instance, in this peer comparison, we see the dividend yield for STRW is nearly 5%, which puts in on the higher end of this peer group:

However, in terms of being a proven dividend grower, growth data shows it does not yet have a positive 5 or 10 year dividend growth CAGR.

Further, its payout ratio is nearly 94%, and my benchmark from prior articles is 75% or less, with more ideally being under 50%. In this case, a 94% payout ratio could make it seem that it leaves much less wiggle room with cashflow, in the event of a cashflow downturn, so that could pose a downside risk. However, keep in mind there is an IRS rule requiring REITs to distribute at least 90% of earnings back to dividends.

Based on the modest yield and lack of long-term growth data, though, I am going with a very low neutral rating in this category.

Balance Sheet Risk: A Tale of Growth, And Leverage Usage

Balance sheet risk elements I often consider with REITs are debt/equity ratios compared to similar peers, and any credit ratings.

Here is a comparison of the D/E:

The first thing that sticks out is the extremely high D/E of 54.45, making this stock a major outlier to this peer group. It also makes it seem like a greater leverage risk.

We said earlier that the firm has been on an acquisition spree this year, and also is paying more interest, but also to add to the story we can see from balance sheet data that they have been continually increasing their long-term debt burden:

So, within the context of this peer group, it does appear to be a greater leverage risk, and the future downside could come from being overly in debt.

I did not find any major credit rating agencies covering this stock lately, so that is another ambiguity since no one like Fitch or S&P has put an investment-grade rating on this firm recently.

Now, some readers may consider the high leverage a necessary evil to achieve growth, with less reliance on equity issuance and potentially diluting the stock. However, I think a balance should be struck and within the context of similar peers, so in this case I am leaning towards a bearish score in this category as this stock's D/E far exceeds the peer average.

Chart Technical Indicators: Showing A Neutral Pattern Now, After This Fall's Bullish Signal

Here, let's quickly look at an easy technical chart using the concept of golden crosses and death crosses:

Based on common industry practice and knowledge, I indicated buy signals (golden crosses) with green circles, as 50-day SMA crosses above 200-day. Red circles were sell signals, 50-day crossing below 200-day. Because the two moving averages have been running mostly parallel lately (which I showed with the blue arrow) I would call this a hold, as there is no clear buy or sell signal now, but neutral momentum.

Because I am mostly a fundamental analyst and not focusing a lot on technicals, this section is included as an additional angle to consider at least some technical aspects in addition to fundamentals. If you would like to see other technical indicators mentioned, let me know in the comments.

Valuation vs Future Upside: Trading Well Above Book Value, With Minimal Upside Forecasted

Lastly, I considered P/FFO and P/B valuation metrics, as well as future price targets.

Here is my valuation comp worksheet, using peer data from Seeking Alpha:

You will quickly notice the extremely high Price/Book valuation, and I believe that this is due to a very depressed book value due to extremely high leverage, so it is wildly skewing the P/B this way, making it appear much more overvalued than the peer average.

For instance, you can glance at the balance sheet data to see book value per share is only $1.09 as of the quarter ending September, so the most recent share price is around 12x book value, which is quite the premium.

Also, the undervaluation shown for forward P/FFO is likely reflecting the fact that for FY25 there is a consensus estimate of -83% YoY FFO decline, followed by just +6.8% growth next year. So, it is not necessarily a classic "undervaluation opportunity" because the FFO decline risk is there. Although the company had some positive guidance that I mentioned earlier, I also talked about rising interest expenses and a heavy debt load.

Using that P/FFO number, I made the following price target worksheet and compared with the avg. Wall St price target:

In addition, another financial website reported that Cantor Fitzgerald had initiated coverage this October with an overweight rating and a $14 price target, which is actually just slightly above the two targets shown in my worksheet above, implying around a 6% upside.

Thus, the stock is justifiably undervalued with a very small upside potential (under 10%) so I think this adds more to it being a neutral than a buy.

Challenge to My Rating: Tenant/Operator Risk Exposure

REITs often can face a major future downside risk and that is what if a major tenant or operator faces financial troubles and struggles to pay rent?

That scenario could impact my hold rating, making it overly optimistic. For instance, from the Q3 investor deck, we see in the chart below that base rent is exposed heavily to the Infinity group.

So, the future downside risk could come if Infinity faces a major financial difficulty. This can be referred to as tenant concentration risk.

Conclusion: Agree With Quant Rating

To wrap up, my rating today agrees with the quant rating of hold, while being more cautious than the bullish consensus from Wall st and SA analysts. However, the last SA analyst covered this REIT in September.

Today's article has shown why the potential impact from high leverage could weigh down on bullishness.

My call to action in this case is to consider the valuable market position this REIT could have in meeting the future demand for skilled nursing and senior care properties, as the population ages and that becomes more necessary, but also consider some of the risks I highlighted and whether they warrant further analysis on downside potential.

data.title: Realty Income: The Strategy Is Sound, But The Shares Are Fully Valued
data.description:
Introduction and Previous Article

In April of this year, we published an article on Realty Income Corporation (O) and issued a hold recommendation. A principal consideration for us at the time was the narrow investment spread for Realty Income’s business. We also opined in the article that because of the economic environment, investor appetite for net-lease assets was likely to be muted. In this article, while we maintain our hold recommendation, we would like to note that investor appetite for net-lease assets is strong. We offer some color on this dynamic and how it might be shaping Realty Income’s strategy and why we still maintain a hold on the stock.

After providing some context on Realty Income’s operating environment, we offer our readers a fair value estimate of the current shares. Our valuation is based on the net asset value per share (NAVPS) and we outline our thinking behind each input used in the framework. Based on this, our fair value estimate range is between $56.65 and $59.18, which is not too far on either side from the current quotation, implying a minimal margin of safety.

Macro Spread

From Exhibit 1, for the last ten years, the spread between the U.S. 10-year Treasury and implied cap rate of the Nareit Free Standing REIT sector has been, on average, 324 basis points. The Q3 2024 implied cap rate for the sector came in at 6.21%, and the U.S. 10-year Treasury is sitting at 4.14% at the time of this writing, implying a spread of 207 basis points, materially narrower than the historical average. The narrower than usual spread suggests that sector is overvalued because investors in the sector are only getting a return of 2.07% above the U.S. 10-year Treasury when they should be getting something in the neighborhood of 3.24% above the U.S. 10-year Treasury. Realty Income makes up 66% of the Nareit Free Standing REIT sector in terms of equity market capitalization. Other prominent, investment-grade-rated REITs in the sector include: Agree Realty Corporation (ADC), Essential Properties Realty Trust, Inc. (EPRT), and NNN REIT, Inc. (NNN). Many of the constituents of the Nareit Free Standing REIT sector are also characterized as net-lease REITs. A net-lease REIT is one in which the REIT leases the property to tenants on a triple net-lease basis. The tenant is responsible for all property-related costs, including insurance, real estate taxes, and utilities.

Source: Nareit REITWatch, CNBC

The narrower than usual spread has not escaped Realty Income. Not only is Realty Income a major component of the sector, but they are also a major player in the sector, as highlighted by their acquisition of Spirit Realty Capital. When spreads are narrow, the current portfolio is valued at a premium, but it becomes more costly and less accretive to engage in acquisitions. External growth is an important part of a net-lease REIT’s strategy. Without it, the growth rate is tethered to the modest escalator embedded in the lease agreement, which is typically based on inflation. By legislative design, REITs cannot retain earnings and so have to keep accessing public debt and equity to fund external growth.

In a sense, when spreads are narrower than usual, net-lease REITs are caught in a tricky situation. They would like to grow through accretive acquisitions, but if spreads are narrow, then the level of accretion is scant and the cost of debt is relatively expensive. If they don’t engage in acquisition activity, then their stock almost becomes an inflation-hedged, fixed-income instrument with minimal growth premium attached.

Why are Spreads Narrow?

One of the dynamics that Realty Income’s CEO has highlighted in the last few earnings calls is that there is an increase in interest for net-lease assets from private capital firms.

BlackRock, Inc.’s (BLK) recent acquisition of ElmTree Funds, Starwood Property Trust, Inc.’s (STWD) recent acquisition of Fundamental Income Properties, LLC and Blackstone Mortgage Trust’s (BXMT) recent entry into the net-lease space underscore increased private fund appetite for the sector. The economic implication of the increased interest in the space is straightforward. If there are more buyers of net-lease assets than sellers of net-lease assets, are in a relatively better bargaining position. This would translate to tighter investment spreads in the marketplace.

Given this market dynamic, how is Realty Income positioning itself?

There are three facets to how Realty Income is navigating the current economic landscape that we wish to highlight. Two of which they have been doing for some time and one, the launching of the Core Plus Fund, is a recent development.

Pivot to Europe

Please see Tables 1 and 2.

Table 1: Realty Income's Portfolio Geographic Diversification
% of ABR*
United States 82.3%
United Kingdom 13.9%
Continental Europe 3.8%
Table 2: Investment Activity for Realty Income for Q3 2025
($ in millions) Initial weighted cash yield
Total U.S. Investment volume $ 380.0 7.0%
Total Europe volume $ 982.8 8.0%
Sum $ 1,362.80

Source: Realty Income Quarterly Investor Presentation, November 2025, Q3 2025 Earnings Supplement. *ABR is annual base rent.

Realty Income has 82.3% of their asset base in the U.S., but for Q3 2025, 72% of investment volume was in Europe. Realty Income can earn 100 basis points more in Europe than in the U.S., as measured by the initial weighted cash yield.

Investment volume activity includes acquiring real estate assets, developing real estate assets, and investing in credit instruments (i.e., making loans).

Earning 100 basis points more on the revenue side of the ledger is just one dimension. Euro-denominated debt is also cheaper. Please see Table 3 for a comparison of two fixed-income instruments that Realty Income has outstanding.

Table 3: Realty Income Fixed Income Data
Coupon Maturity Denomination Yield to Maturity
5.125% 2/15/2034 USD 4.73%

5.125%

 7/6/2034 EUR 3.86%

Here is the CEO of Realty Income summing up the rationale for investing in Europe.

“The European investment opportunity remains compelling, driven by a fragmented competitive landscape, a larger total addressable market than the United States, and a current cost of debt for euro-denominated 10-year notes that is approximately 100 basis points inside of U.S. dollar costs.”

Source: Q3 2025 Earnings Call, TradingView

In Table 4, we present Realty Income’s debt summary by currency for context.

Table 4: Realty Income Debt Summary by Currency ($ in millions)
USD $18,493.0 63.0%
EUR $3,212.4 10.9%
GBP $7,632.2 26.0%

Note that assets in Continental Europe account for 3.8% of ABR (from Table 1), and yet Euro denominated debt is 10.9% of total debt. There is a clear pivot to Europe in terms of the current deal volume and securing Euro-denominated debt financing.

Credit Investments

Given their strong balance sheet and A3/A- issuer rating by Moody’s and S&P, Realty Income has access to debt at a lower cost than many firms. For firms that do not have access to cheap debt or find it onerous to secure a bank loan, Realty Income could be a source of capital. Realty Income’s rationale for extending credit is that it brings in a higher yield today, and it could potentially lead to a sale-leaseback transaction with the counterparty down the line. Please see Table 5. Credit investments are in the “other investments” category.

Table 5: Q3 2025 Investment Volume Breakdown
($ in millions) % Initial weighted cash yield
Acquisitions 830.30 60.9% 7.1%
Real estate development 148.30 10.9% 7.4%
Other investments 384.20 28.2% 9.0%

Source: S&P, Moody’s

Realty Income’s recent preferred equity investment in the real estate of CityCenter Las Vegas, which should earn it an unlevered internal rate of return in the low to mid 8s fits in this mold.

Core Plus Fund

If it is not an opportune time to be buyers of net-lease assets because of increased competition in the U.S., then one way to take advantage of the current economic dynamic is to be a seller of net-lease assets.

Realty Income did sell some net-lease assets in Q3, but that is not the activity we are referring to here. Realty Income buys and sells assets every quarter, and it is part of their capital recycling strategy. However, by introducing the perpetual life U.S. Open-End Core Plus Fund, Realty Income can sell equity in the fund, earn recurring fee income, and still potentially retain control. Nifty move.

There is some economic basis for introducing the Fund. As Realty Income noted in their November 2024 presentation, their beta statistic has increased in recent years. Please see Exhibit 2.

From 2015 to 2019, Realty Income’s beta statistic was 0.4 and since 2020 has been at 0.9. The market has a beta statistic of 1.0, and it is not surprising that, as a company grows, its beta statistic will trend towards 1.0. Having a lower beta statistic is desirable because it means that the stock is less volatile. Less volatility increases the appeal of a stock to investors who take a portfolio perspective. Adding a lower beta stock could increase the risk-adjusted returns of a portfolio as measured by the Treynor ratio.

If Realty Income were to expand their investor pool to private capital investors, it could lower the beta statistic because private capital investors are perceived to transact less frequently and hold on to the investment for a longer time horizon.

Another rationale for the Core Plus Fund vehicle is that it will increase returns because of the ability to earn fee income. Please see Exhibit 3.

Currently the seed capital Realty Income contributed to the fund is $1.4 billion, and, in September, they held an initial closing of $716 million. Investors included the Townsend Group along with public and corporate pension funds, asset managers, and investment advisors. Realty Income’s enterprise value is about $81 billion.

A third rationale for the private capital move is that this puts Realty Income on a path to perhaps being valued as an asset manager and not just a net-lease REIT. Asset Managers are valued at a premium to publicly traded REITs. Please see Exhibit 4.

There is an item we are keeping tabs on. The seed portfolio of the Core Plus Fund came from Realty Income’s balance sheet. We would like clarity on how the decision to move assets from the balance sheet into the Core Plus Fund is carried out. As an example, 183 income generating properties with half of ABR derived from industrial properties were included in the fund, and the remainder were mostly retail properties. Basically a 50-50 split between industrial and retail assets. This appears to be out of proportion to the current portfolio. As per their September 2025 Fact Sheet, 14.7% of their portfolio was characterized as industrial and 79.8% were characterized as retail. Retail properties are generally higher yielding and lower growth compared to industrial properties. More disclosure on this would be welcome.

Valuation

Please see Table 6.

Table 6: Realty Income Valuation NAVPS ($ in thousands except per share info)
Annualized p.f. adjusted EBITDAre Q3 2025 $5,384,440
Growth rate 6.33%
Next year annualized p.f. adjusted EBITDAre $5,725,109.62
Cap rate 7.10%
Gross asset value $80,635,346.70
Debt $28,371,757.00
Net asset value $ 52,263,589.70
Shares Outstanding 922,575
Net asset value per share (NAVPS) $ 56.65

We offer our readers a valuation framework and start with the annualized pro forma adjusted EBITDAre (henceforth referred to as EBITDAre) as disclosed in their recent supplemental. We then incorporate the growth rate of the EBITDAre figure from Q3 2022 to the present after considering share issuance. This is our next year’s adjusted EBTIDAre forecast removing the impact of share issuance. The time period selected for the growth rate excludes the post-Covid monetary easing regime and is better representative of the current macro environment than using a longer time horizon, in our view.

The cap rate selected is reflective of real estate acquisitions across all geographies for the year-to-date for Realty Income. We ignore cap rate for credit investments and development activities because these are not core activities for Realty Income. The cap rate selected is also 100 basis points above the long-term cost of equity for Realty Income based on their disclosure.

Deducting debt from the gross asset value and then subsequently dividing the result by current shares outstanding gives us a price estimate not too far from the current share price, implying that the shares are fairly valued.

If you were to repeat the exercise and instead of using the year-to-date acquisition cap rate of 7.1%, instead use the year-to-date disposition cap rate of 6.9% you would get a fair value estimate of $59.18.

Please see Exhibit 5 for the price action of Realty Income's stock for the last six months. The two horizontal bars reflect the high and low range of our estimate.

Risks/Limitations

There are limitations to our framework, and we wish to highlight three of them. First is that Exhibit 1 presented unlagged implied cap rates. A reasonable argument could be made that it takes time for implied cap rates to adjust to changes to the U.S.10-year Treasury and some lag should have been incorporated. For our part, we did not find a statistically significant benefit to incorporating a lag.

Secondly, our valuation is rudimentary. A more elaborate NAVPS valuation framework might project the fee income earned from the Core Plus Fund and/or incorporate a view on the level and direction of interest rates in 2026. We believe that it is still early days for the Core Plus Fund and given the size relative to the enterprise value of Realty Income currently, the impact is likely to be de minimis for some time. On a more general note, we prefer using inputs that require the fewest possible assumptions.

One last item we would like to point out is that we did not incorporate a management premium. Analysts often incorporate a premium (or discount) in a NAVPS framework to account for management track record. Morningstar gives Realty Income’s capital allocation stewardship an exemplary rating, and we can see some justification for adding a modest premium, though we leave that decision to our reader's judgment.

data.title: Micron Is In A 'Supercycle' - Q1 Results Might Not Be Enough
data.description:
Investment Thesis

As Micron Technology (NASDAQ:MU) gets ready to report its Q1 FY26 ER next week, a $283 billion dollar question is bubbling up to the surface—is HBM a cyclical commodity? The answer will be the difference in justifying the additional 200B in market cap Micron’s shares have added since April this year, and the Q1 ER next week will be judgement day for the American memory maker.

As bulls and bears debate intensely over HBM being a cyclical commodity or whether Micron’s memory business is still in a boom/bust cycle, the company’s shares are ripping through the markets. Last week’s announcement about Micron exiting from its Crucial consumer business will pour more fuel on the debate about its cyclical business, with more clarifications expected next week.

While my outlook for Micron is strong, I believe the shares are running red hot into its Q2 ER. Therefore, I am downgrading my views on Micron to a Hold but next week’s ER is sure going to be intense.

Micron Is Benefiting From a Memory Supercycle

When I first wrote about Micron last year, I rated it as a Strong Buy and was struck by management’s discipline and intense focus to penetrate the HBM market versus the opportunity that HBM and data center TAM presented to the company. I even predicted that 2025 would be a great year for memory and HBM and prices would rise, unlike prior predictions of slowing memory prices this year. I did follow the coverage up with my thesis in July this year, which showed Micron was “scorching HBM competition.”

With the latest numbers that I have, I see that Micron has extended its lead in the HBM market by 330 bp to now hold 25.7% market share in the HBM market, achieving a target they had aimed for next year.

What helped them so far in FY25 was the rapid growth in their HBM business, which resulted in the company scoring $8B in annualized HBM revenues, accounting for ~21% of Micron’s FY25 revenues of $37.4B. Through FY25, Micron benefitted from a ramp in HBM3, particularly from HBM3E. Also, what helped Micron immensely so far this year was that Samsung, a larger industry peer, was unable to get their HBM3 memory products qualified for Nvidia’s Blackwell GPUs due to thermal issues. Since then, Samsung has been able to qualify its HBM3E for Blackwell, which might slow down the almost-duopolistic benefits Micron and the industry leader, SK Hynix, benefited from in the HBM3E ramp phase.

Note: SK Hynix, Samsung, and Micron (in that order) form the famed troika of the world’s largest three memory suppliers.

At a macro level what may have definitely been helping the entire memory industry is the surging memory prices, which have skyrocketed by 172% so far this year alone. On top of that, bit shipments are expected to jump ~25% or more this year, leading to a potent mix of rising ASPs and rising bit shipments. The unit economics of the memory business could never be better.

This probably explains why Micron’s management decided to exit from its Crucial consumer business and focus all resources entirely on the data center component of its business. The DC business now accounts for 56% of Micron’s overall revenues at a 52% gross margin. Also note that Micron’s DC business gross margins are higher than its overall business, which recorded ~45% gross margins in Q4 FY25 and ~40% gross margins through FY25. Expanding gross margins is a very good sign that portends further acceleration in Micron’s business and improving factory utilization.

Investors can also see how Micron is now holding inventory on the balance sheet for just 125 days, dramatically lower than +150 days of inventory two years ago. At the very least, I am expecting some marginal slowdown in gross margin expansion, but don’t be surprised if gross margins still expand from here on out in the report next week.

In addition to these developments surrounding Micron’s business economics, investors will be focused on two additional key elements of its Q1 ER.

First, markets will be wanting to hear the magic words “sold out” when it comes to Micron’s forward HBM production capacity. Last quarter, management stopped short of saying their supply was sold out. Instead, management said that they “expect to conclude agreements to sell out the remainder of our total HBM calendar 2026 supply in the coming months.” That was based on the development that pricing for HBM3E was largely locked in while “active discussions” were still ongoing with customers for HBM4 volume, with first HBM4 shipments expected to begin some time in Q2 CY26. Next week, markets will be eagerly hoping to hear these magic words.

Since Micron’s Q4 quarter in September, a slate of billion-dollar mega deals involving GPUs and XPUs for various data centers have been announced, which has surely expanded the HBM TAM for Micron. Expectations for Micron’s FY26 revenues have already surged. Consensus estimates that can be found on Seeking Alpha show that FY26 revenue expectations have surged 8% from September this year to now expect 54% growth in FY26 revenues of $57.4B.

The second element markets will be interested in looking at is Micron’s capex guidance, which, per the last known guidance, stands at ~$18B. If I factor in September’s revenue expectations, that implies ~34% of FY26 revenues. But since FY26 revenue expectations have moved up by 8%, I expect management will raise their FY26 capex guidance by at least ~$1.5B to $19-20B.

What will really move Micron’s shares is if management significantly raises capex to match the prior years’ average of ~36% of revenues, which would be significantly bullish for Micron.

Last month, in an investor conference, Micron’s CFO, Mark Murphy, did hint that the $18B capex guidance would be revised in next week’s Q1 ER:

There's going to be pressure on that to come up this year. So I would expect at our earnings call, which is about a month from now, we would probably -- we will bring that up and update you at that time.

So investors can definitely get some capex guidance updates next week.

But staying on point, Murphy’s answer about expecting forward capex guidance was in response to a question by the analyst who asked whether Micron’s business would continue to grow beyond the 8-10 quarter uptrend cycle in its memory business. Murphy’s response to the question, in addition to his capex hints, was that the overall memory supply that will be shipped to markets “now and beyond '26 is going to be inadequate” to meet demand. Much of that demand will come from the markets transitioning from 12-stack HBM3 and HBM3Es to 16-stack HBM4s. That signals a strong outlook beyond the traditional 8-10 quarterly business upcycles, and Micron is preparing to benefit from this uptrend.

In my opinion, Micron may be at the start of a supercycle, no doubt. The company is already starting to see the accretive benefits of being adequately invested, allowing it to be at the right place at the right time. And next week’s Q1 quarter is likely to be a strong show of performance for Micron. However, as I demonstrate below, markets have already bid up Micron’s shares to a level that is ahead of Q1 expectations, as noted below.

Micron Is Trading Ahead Of Expectations.

There is no easy way to value Micron. Due to the past cyclical nature of Micron’s business, markets typically trend the company’s book value to look for tops in Micron’s boom leg of the cyclical business. So far, Micron’s shares trade at an extremely elevated multiple to its book value of 5.5x, a level last seen in the dot-com era.

This clearly shows that markets have gone past the boom/bust cycle theory for Micron and are expecting the company to be in some sort of supercycle or that Micron’s business is finally secular rather than cyclical.

To value Micro for next year, I have simply resorted to analyzing its revenue trends and expectations to observe the market's perception of Micron’s share price estimate.

If investors look at FY23 and FY24 forward revenue estimates for Micron below, they will see that Micron was trading at ~3.4-3.5x EV/forward revenues for average revenue growth of ~44-45% in those two years. In both those years, FY24 and FY25, Micron eventually went on to deliver 62% revenue growth in FY24 and ~49% revenue growth in FY25.

Using those past expectations as a base, and our prior assumptions of Micron’s $57B of expected revenues in FY26 implying 53-54% growth, Micron should be valued at ~4.5x FY26 revenues. However, markets are valuing Micron at 5.1x FY26 revenues, implying markets are expecting Micron to eventually deliver ~63% revenue growth this year, 10% higher than consensus estimates for FY26 revenues of ~$57B.

This means Micron is more than priced for perfection as it enters Q1 results next week and it makes little sense to buy Micron ahead of earnings. There may be pullbacks coming which will offer investors to increase exposure to Micron.

While I believe the strong demand will boost Micron’s FY26 revenues eventually, I do not expect that to occur in Q1 alone and believe markets have gotten too optimistic at current levels. This is why I am downgrading Micron to a Hold despite strong AI prospects for Micron.

Risks To Thesis & What To Look For In Q1 ER

What markets will be looking for in Q1 next week:

  1. Q1 Expectations: $3.87 EPS on $12.8B revenues, 47% growth with gross margins of ~51.5%
  2. Q2 Expectations: $4.24 EPS on $13.8B revenues, 71% growth
  3. FY26 capex guidance of $18B
  4. Is Micron's HBM supply for FY26, specifically HBM4 sold out?
  5. Bit shipments to rise in low-mid teens

If Micron over-delivers on any of these metrics, especially on its gross margins and capex guidance, expect strong gains in Micron’s shares.

Takeaway

Micron is going to have a busy Q1 call next week as it looks to further solidify the end of the debate about its memory business continuing to benefit from a super cycle.

While the long-term business tailwinds continue to support the growth thesis for Micron, the shares have run up significantly in the last few months, and investors could wait for better entry points.

I am downgrading Micron to a Hold ahead of its Q1 earnings.

data.title: Align Technology: Attractive Entry After Q3 Beat And Multiple Compression
data.description:
Investment Thesis

Align Technology (ALGN) shares, in my opinion, are now noticeably oversold, and in terms of multiples they are trading at a clear discount to historical averages. I wrote about the company almost six months ago, and in my conclusion I recommended buying shares only after a 10-15% correction from that price ($180 on June 19). The stock price is currently holding at the levels I recommended buying at.

In addition, the company issued an unexpectedly good report for Q3 (after a disastrous Q2), ahead of market expectations and management forecasts, and gave hope for an early recovery in demand for Align products. Based on this, I am changing my Hold recommendation to Buy with a target price of $180 and a 9.4% growth potential to the current price (at the time of writing, $164.58).

Growth in Volume, Pressure on ASPs

As I said, in July I recommended buying stocks only after a correction of 10-15% or more. The shares seemed expensive to me, there was no growth potential for them according to the results of the DCF model assessment, and the growth drivers were controversial. As a result, the stock plummeted by more than 30% in July after the release of a weak second-quarter report. Revenue then declined and fell short of market expectations, and net profit also did not reach the expected level. At that moment, the stock could be bought for $130-140 and these were really very interesting levels. In my opinion, the shares are still interesting to buy, especially since the reporting for the third quarter turned out to be better than expected and gave hope to the market and to all of us as a whole for a recovery in demand. Let's take a closer look at the report.

Revenue increased by 1.8% YoY to $995.7, which is above the upper threshold of the market consensus forecast and management forecasts ($965-985 million). There are several important points regarding revenue that I would like to mention. Firstly, Invisalign's core product, transparent aligners, has become the main driver for growth. During the quarter, about 647.8 thousand sets of these aligners were sold, which is 4.9% more y/y. The demand for treatment for children increased strongly – sales increased by +8.3% y/y (or +14.7% QoQ), reaching 256 thousand. In other words, we can already see that sales volume is higher than revenue growth, as Clear Aligner's revenue grew by only +2.4% YoY.

This leads to the second important point – the average price for a set of aligners in Q3 was $5 lower than in Q2, and the average receipt was about $1,245. Management attributes the price drop to discounts and lower prices for new markets. In other words, in order to enter new markets with less developed economies and low solvency, companies have to sell cheaper.

The low price was partially offset by a favorable exchange rate, because the main demand for aligners came from Europe, Asia and Latin America, while stagnation continues in the United States.

The management explains the restrained demand in the United States by the fact that in the United States, most patients postpone treatment due to high costs and economic uncertainty, and all Align's steps to stimulate demand through marketing have so far been futile. The Imaging Systems & CAD/CAM segment showed a slight 0.6% drop in revenue y/y, but this was expected, as Q3 is traditionally considered a weak period for the segment – dentists mostly buy all these scanners in the summer.

In terms of operating margin, the GAAP and adjusted figures are quite different. The drop in GAAP operating margin from 16% to 9.7% is due to the fact that the calculation included many one-time items of expenses and write-offs related to the restructuring of the company. Without this, the total operating margin is about 23.9% versus 22.1%. Let me remind you that in the Q2 press release, management predicted about 22% of the operating margin in Q3. This restructuring is being carried out to optimize and increase business efficiency, and it is clear that this is yielding results. In addition, the favorable exchange rate helped. As soon as the restructuring process is completed, the GAAP margin will both grow and adjust to the adjusted result.

Obviously, according to GAAP, net income will be much lower than last year due to the same write-downs. It is more correct to focus on the adjusted result, according to which net profit was $189.0 million (+7.6% y/y), and adjusted EPS was $2.61 (+11% y/y). EPS growth was primarily driven by an increase in adjusted operating profit and aggressive share buybacks, which the company had just stepped up after a sharp drop in the share price in the summer. Over the year, it decreased from about 74.8 million to 72.4 million, that is, by almost 3%. The consensus expected about $2.42, and the actual result was 8% higher. Against this background, the shares soared by 11%.

Overall, if we summarize everything that happened during the quarter, then demand from international markets and increased demand for children's treatment supported the sale of aligners, but at a lower price than in Q2, while demand in the United States remained sluggish. This did not prevent the company from increasing adjusted results in terms of operating profit, net profit and EPS, however, a large number of write-downs and one-time restructuring costs reduced its GAAP margin. It turns out to be a volatile year for the company – from quarter to quarter, the results are either much better than expected, or much worse. The fourth quarter will determine a lot for 2025, so let's look at management's forecasts and speculate on what to expect from the last three months of this year.

Align Sets Up for Strong Q4 Finish

The management's view of Q4 is rather positive. According to their scenario, revenue will grow by 3-5% y/y in the range of $1.025–1.045 billion due to the fact that demand in international markets will remain high, while in the United States it will finally recover and, accordingly, the sales volume of aligners will grow, as well as sales in the Imaging Systems & CAD/CAM segment. As a result, annual revenue will be approximately at the level of last year ($4.0–4.05 billion) due to the weak first two quarters. Adjusted operating margin in Q4 will grow by 2% QoQ to 26%, and GAAP will increase to 15.3–15.8% from 9.7%. The growth drivers will be overall margin growth, increased demand in the United States and regions with higher average receipts, as well as cost reductions and the absence of those write–offs that were in Q3. In total, management expects a 13.6-13.8% GAAP operating margin by the end of the year, which is certainly lower than last year, but not this year. there were so many write-offs. And according to Non-Gaap, the company expects to achieve the operating margin target set at the beginning of the year at 22.5%. Overall, management's expectations are close to consensus forecasts: Wall Street expects revenue growth in Q4 of 3.68% to $1.03 billion, and adjusted EPS of $2.96 (+21.43%). For the whole year, the market expects revenue growth of only 0.77% to $4.03 billion, and EPS of $10.19 (+9.24%).

Nevertheless, I believe that Align has every chance to at least show results at the upper limit of its forecast, and at most the results may turn out to be even better than expected. Since we see high demand in international markets, among teenagers, and if demand starts to revive in the United States, then in Q4 it is quite possible to see revenue at the level of $ 1.045-1.055 billion. Moreover, the company plans to further increase the average receipt, which was lower in Q3. With the growth of sales and the average receipt, cost control, complete absence of interest expenses, and aggressive share buybacks due to the low price, it is quite possible to see adjusted EPS of about $2.80–2.90. This means that by the end of the year, Align will earn about $10.05-10.20 Adjusted EPS. As the Fed has moved to ease monetary policy and interest rates are slowly falling, a gradual acceleration in economic growth could really boost sales in the US – and this is a key moment for growth in Q4 and in 2026.

Undervalued by Any Reasonable Metric

In this chapter, I will talk about my DCF model, its prerequisites, and final evaluation. Let me remind you that I calculate the target share price by forecasting the unlevered FCF for the period 2025-2030, discount the forecast FCFS, then calculate the terminal value by multiplying the forward EV/EBITDA and P/E by EBITDA and net profit of the last forecast year (2030) and also discount. I add up the discounted FCF and Terminal Value to get the total value of the business (when calculated using P/E, this is immediately the Market cap. with EV/EBITDA, this is the Enterprise value from which Net Debt is also subtracted), which I divide by the number of shares and get the projected fair value of one share.

I have slightly revised the assumptions that I put into the financial model six months ago. Now, the average annual revenue growth rate for 2025-2030 will be about 6.15%, compared to almost 9% in that version of the model. This is primarily due to the revision of revenue expectations for 2025 – I was more positive at the time, but due to the weak second quarter, I had to revise my forecast in favor of 1% growth (it was 3.66%). Well, in general, weaker demand from the United States will recover more slowly than expected according to my financial model. As for the operating margin, I am already more positive here – I expect the operating margin to grow from 17% in 2024 to 21% by 2030 due to the restructuring carried out by the company and the growth of the average check faster than inflation. Capital expenditures will be in the range of 3-5% of revenue, which is the average value for Align over the past 5 years. This is generally enough to maintain and develop production. As a result, the average FCF Yield in 2025-2030 will be about 14.40% (in the previous model it was 13.28%) against 13.34% in 2020-2024.

According to my calculations, the WACC is about 12% (all the prerequisites are shown in the table above).

With the fall in stock prices, almost all the multiples were adjusted. Currently, the company is trading at a discount both to its counterparts and to the five-year average values. Moreover, the discounts to their averages are 40-60%. The stock clearly looks undervalued right now.

Based on the results of the DCF model, in the baseline scenario, stocks have a growth potential of 9-10% to the current price (at the time of writing, $164.58). Under more optimistic conditions (multiples rising and WACC falling), the growth potential reaches up to 19-20%, but in a negative scenario there is no upside or downside. Based on the basic scenario of my financial model, I predict the stock will grow to $180 over the next year, which will give investors a return of about 10%. My rating is Buy.

I would like to note that my forecast almost coincides with the average target price of Wall Street analysts. But at the same time, ratings vary – almost half recommend Hold, and the other half immediately recommend Strong Buy.

Buyback Firepower Intact Through 2028

Align has almost no debt burden, and interest costs are zero. At the end of Q3, the company had about $1.00 billion in cash in its accounts, and the total debt was about $121 million. Accordingly, the net debt is negative.

Align does not pay dividends, and encourages shareholders only by repurchasing shares. In April, another $1 billion was authorized for repurchases until 2028. As of the end of September, about $71.6 million had been spent under this program, meaning there were still $928.4 million available funds for repurchases. In the third quarter, the company repurchased about 500,000 shares at an average price of $136.77 and for a total amount of $68 million. In August, management announced an acceleration of the buyback: the OMR (open market repurchase) program was launched for $ 200 million until January 2026. About $128.4 million will be allocated for buybacks in the fourth quarter of 2025. This will strongly support quarterly EPS. As a result, over the past year (from September 2024 to September 2025), the number of shares decreased by 3%.

Risks

The main risk is associated with sluggish demand in the United States. Align is very dependent on the macroeconomic situation in the United States, because if the economy is in recession, high interest rates and consumption are falling, and savings are growing, then the company's sales of aligners are falling. If the rate-cutting cycle does not continue next year, demand may remain weak and financial results will be worse than expected. I consider this risk unlikely, as the Fed has clearly taken a course towards easing monetary policy. Currency fluctuations can negatively affect business margins and revenue levels – this is another risk. Since the company generates almost half of its revenue abroad, it is unprofitable for it to strengthen the dollar – revenue is lower because of this. There is also a risk of high competition, especially in the North American market. Competition forces us to make frequent discounts and sell cheaper in order to maintain market share, and this negatively affects the average receipt and further financial results. All other risks are related to geopolitical tensions (trade wars and tariffs), possible unsuccessful investments, business seasonality, and so on.

Final Thoughts

Six months ago, I wrote that Align is a good margin business, but it is expensive and it is better to wait for the price correction. Now this correction has happened, so I remain consistent and recommend the stock to buy. I believe that consumption and economic growth in the United States will accelerate in 2026 with a gradual reduction in rates, and this will support demand in North America. I expect the stock price to rise by 10% over the horizon of the year with a target price of $180 and change the rating from Hold to Buy.

data.title: Capital Gains SZN Heats Up: What Investors Could Expect
data.description:
The year is winding down, but Cap Gains SZN is ramping up. It’s that time when the holidays bring together family, friends and shared traditions. There is a natural pause to reflect on the year’s accomplishments and lessons learned. It also marks the period when fund families start to calculate their net realized capital gains. For investors, “lessons learned” can take on a new meaning as they start to see what their tax bill could look like.

Around this time, most mutual fund and exchange-traded fund (ETF) providers release estimates of their upcoming distributions. We track and aggregate these early numbers to help you better prepare for what to expect. While the amounts won’t be final until distributions are officially announced and paid, with more than 75% of all funds having their estimates announced at this point, there is enough information to start assessing the broader picture.

Reflecting on 2025 market returns & historical distributions

Despite volatility early in the year, year-to-date market performance remains strong across the board. Focusing on equities, the Russell 3000 Index is up roughly 17.4% through the end of November, while international developed (MSCI EAFE Index) and emerging markets (MSCI EM Index) are up 24.5% and 29.5%, respectively, through the same time frame. So, the tradition of investors running into capital gains distributions is expected to continue and can potentially be a bit more widespread across asset classes given broader participation outside of U.S. equities.

As shown in the chart below, capital gains are distributed annually—typically during the month of December—and happen regardless of how markets perform. In strong market years, like this one, a tax bill alongside positive performance seems like a natural trade-off. Strong performance adds pressure, but it’s the investment activity within the funds that causes taxable distributions.

Having a highly appreciated portfolio—something that many investors may have experienced lately—makes it harder for managers to incorporate active management decisions without tapping gains. That challenge grows when investor preferences lead to outflows, which can pressure managers to sell positions they otherwise wouldn’t touch. This is also why capital gains may still occur in down years.

The table below offers a snapshot of how this translates in dollar terms. We incorporated the average capital gain distribution paid by U.S. equity funds over the last 4 years (2021-2024) along with this year’s estimate of 8.1%. Assuming a $500k portfolio at the start of each year and applying the highest long-term capital gains tax rate (23.8%), that comes to roughly $6-$12k in tax each year. If this year plays out as estimated, that’s nearly $50k in taxes over the last 5 years.

2025 – The broad view

With that backdrop in mind, it makes sense to start the broader asset-class discussion with U.S. equities. They tend to command the largest share of most investor portfolios and have also delivered some of the strongest multi-year returns across asset classes, which means they typically drive the bulk of capital gain distributions. This year is no exception, as U.S. equity funds are expected to make the largest distribution relative to other major fund categories, averaging about 8.1%.

This isn’t much of a surprise, as the Russell 3000 Index has delivered returns of +20% in five of the last six years and is up 17.4% year-to-date, so most funds are sitting on some hefty, embedded gains. Below, you will see a summary of distribution estimates within the U.S. equity category, broken down in style box form.

First, across market cap, estimated distributions are coming in slightly higher across the board. Large cap funds are averaging 7%-10%, mid-cap 7%-11% and small cap 6% through 8%. Growth-oriented strategies are toward the top of each market cap segment.

Most categories are estimating distributions roughly 1 to 3 percent higher than what they paid out in 2024. Mid-cap growth stands out in particular; after paying the highest distribution among all U.S. equity segments last year, they are estimating an increase of about 3 percent for 2025.

Outside of U.S. equities, international equity estimates are coming in a bit higher than we have been used to, at 7.2% on average. This is partially due to their strong 2025 returns. The largest estimates are seen within smaller and more growth-oriented segments. Sector equity is coming in modestly lower at roughly 6% on average, with a wide range. Growth-leaning sectors are at the higher end, aligning with U.S. style-box trends, whereas real assets and defensive sectors are on the lower side.

Non-traditional equity funds, which include funds focused on derivatives and long-short approaches, are tracking just under 6% on average. Given the nature of these strategies, they tend to be less tax-efficient, so expecting a mid-single-digit average estimate isn’t surprising.

Commodities and alternative funds are estimating to pay a distribution toward the lower end of the overall range, with most distributions classified as short-term rather than long-term. Allocation funds are estimating similar levels, with larger averages coming from more aggressive, equity-heavy mixes, topping out at 5.7% on average.

Summary - Investor and advisor action steps

While we could go much deeper and dissect each asset class, fund family, or individual fund, investors should use this data as a broad starting point. It can help identify areas that may not be aligned with their taxable interests.

Investors may consider not automatically reinvesting distributions if minimizing current-year tax drag is a priority. Instead, consider directing distributions to more tax‑efficient options that align with your objectives and circumstances. One way to pursue after‑tax wealth growth is to reduce tax drag where possible.

Important risk disclosures

METHODOLOGY OF CALCULATING AVERAGE CAPITAL GAINS DISTRIBUTIONS OF FUND CATEGORY: The average capital gain distribution as a % of Net Asset Value (NAV) is calculated using long-term and short-term capital gain distributions as reported by respective fund families. % of NAV is calculated as ($ capital gain estimate ÷ NAV). For funds that report estimates as a range a mid-point is used [(low estimate + high estimate) ÷ 2]. It is important to understand the NAV date used in this calculation vary by fund family based on each company’s formal distribution announcement. Total Number of funds within each US Category Group: 6,629 for U.S. Equity, 3,836 for International Equity, 1,641 for Sector Equity, 636 for Non-Traditional Equity, 157 for Commodities, 5,877 for Allocation, 414 for Alternative. Total Number of funds within each Morningstar Category: 1,170 for Large Value, 1,386 for Large Blend, 1,088 for Large Growth, 423 for Mid-Cap Value, 403 for Mid-Cap Blend, 495 for Mid-Cap Growth, 488 for Small Value, 624 for Small Blend, 552 for Small Growth.

Investments in small cap, micro cap, and companies with capitalization smaller than the Russell 2000® Index, are subject to the risks of common stocks, may experience considerable price fluctuations and are more volatile than large company stocks. Generally, the smaller the company size, the greater the risks.

Non-U.S. markets, which may include developed, emerging, and frontier markets, entail different risks than those typically associated with U.S. markets, including currency fluctuations, political and economic instability, accounting changes and foreign taxation. Non-U.S. securities may be less liquid and more volatile than U.S. securities. The risks associated with non-U.S. securities may be amplified for emerging markets securities. Because frontier markets are among the smallest, least developed, least liquid, and most volatile of the emerging markets, investments in frontier markets are generally subject to a greater risk of loss than investments in developed or traditional emerging markets.

Commodities may have greater volatility than traditional securities. The value of commodities may be affected by changes in overall market movements, changes in interest rates or sectors affecting a particular industry or commodity, and international economic, political and regulatory developments.

Alternative strategies may be subject to risks related to equity securities; fixed income securities; non-U.S. and emerging markets securities; currency trading, which may involve instruments that have volatile prices, are illiquid or create economic leverage; commodity investments; illiquid securities; and derivatives including futures, options, forwards and swaps.

Index Definitions:

Russell 3000® Index: Index measures the performance of the largest 3000 U.S. companies representing approximately 98% of the investable U.S. equity market.

MSCI EAFE (Europe, Australasia, Far East) Index: A free float-adjusted market capitalization index that is designed to measure the equity market performance of developed markets, excluding the U.S. and Canada.

MSCI Emerging Markets Index: A float-adjusted market capitalization index that consists of indices in 24 emerging economies.

Morningstar Fund US Category Group Definition:

U.S. Equity: U.S. equity portfolios invest primarily in stocks of U.S. companies, spanning market-capitalization segments from small cap to large cap and investment styles from value to blend to growth. These strategies focus on companies listed or principally operating in the United States and are grouped by size and valuation characteristics—large, mid, and small categories, each with value, blend, or growth orientations. U.S. equity funds may target broadly diversified exposure across industries or emphasize specific risk and style factors tied to the U.S. stock market.

International Equity: International equity portfolios invest primarily in non-U.S. stocks, including developed and emerging markets. These funds often diversify across countries such as Japan, the U.K., Germany, France, and emerging regions in Asia or Latin America. Categories within this group include foreign large-cap value, blend, and growth funds, as well as foreign small/mid-cap strategies and global allocations that invest primarily outside the United States. These portfolios typically hold less than 20% of assets in U.S. securities.

Sector Equity: Sector-equity portfolios concentrate investments in specific industries or economic sectors, such as technology, health, energy, real estate, communications, consumer cyclical, consumer defensive, natural resources, or financial services. These strategies seek capital appreciation by focusing exposure on companies whose prospects are tied to a particular sector. Because of their narrower focus, sector funds typically exhibit higher volatility and greater sensitivity to industry-specific trends than diversified equity funds.

Nontraditional Equity: Nontraditional-equity portfolios use alternative or derivative-driven strategies rather than straightforward long-only stock selection. These may include long–short equity, derivative-income strategies (such as covered-call overlays), equity-hedged approaches using protective options or market-timing, and defined-outcome structures that use options to cap upside and buffer downside. These funds typically exhibit lower beta to traditional equity markets and seek to manage or reshape equity risk through structured exposures.

Commodities: Commodity portfolios invest in physical commodities or commodity-linked derivatives, such as futures contracts tied to metals, energy, agriculture, livestock, or diversified commodity baskets. Some funds diversify across many commodity sectors, while others concentrate on specific industries such as energy or precious metals. These strategies often serve as inflation hedges or diversifiers because they behave differently from traditional equity and bond markets.

Allocation: Allocation portfolios invest in multiple asset classes — typically stocks, bonds, and cash — seeking a balanced mix of income, growth, and risk control. These strategies differ by the level of equity exposure, ranging from conservative (low equity) to aggressive (high equity), including target-date funds that shift allocations over time. Allocation funds are designed to provide diversified, risk-controlled portfolios appropriate for different investor objectives or time horizons.

Alternative: Alternative portfolios invest using nontraditional investment approaches or exposures beyond traditional stocks and bonds, often including derivatives, event-driven strategies, macro trading, relative-value arbitrage, multistrategy frameworks, or trend-following models. These strategies aim to provide diversification, reduce correlation to traditional markets, exploit market inefficiencies, or generate returns independent of equity and bond market direction. Many alternative strategies use short positions, leverage, or derivatives to reshape portfolio risk.

Morningstar Fund Category Definition:

U.S. Fund - Large Value: Large-value portfolios invest mainly in large U.S. companies that are less expensive or growing more slowly than other large-cap stocks. These portfolios favor companies with low valuations (low price ratios, high dividend yields) and slow growth characteristics. Stocks in the top 70% of the U.S. equity market by capitalization are defined as large cap.

U.S. Fund - Large Blend: Large-blend portfolios favor U.S. firms at the larger end of the market-capitalization range. Some aim to own an array of value and growth stocks while others employ a discipline that leads to holdings with valuations and growth rates close to the large-cap averages. Stocks in the bottom 10% of the capitalization of the U.S. equity market are defined as large cap. The blend style is assigned to portfolios where neither growth nor value characteristics predominate.

U.S. Fund - Large Growth: Large-growth portfolios invest primarily in big U.S. companies expected to grow faster than other large-cap stocks. These portfolios emphasize firms with high growth rates for earnings, sales, book value, and cash flow, which often leads to higher valuations (high price ratios and low dividend yields). Most focus on rapidly expanding industries. Stocks in the top 70% of U.S. market capitalization are considered large cap.

U.S. Fund – Mid-Cap Value: Mid-cap value portfolios invest in medium-sized U.S. companies that are less expensive or growing more slowly than the overall market. These portfolios focus on firms with low valuations and slower growth in earnings, sales, book value, and cash flow. Stocks in the middle 20% of U.S. market capitalization are considered mid-cap.

U.S. Fund – Mid-Cap Blend: Mid-cap blend portfolios invest in U.S. stocks of various sizes and styles, giving them a middle-of-the-road profile. These portfolios typically avoid both high-priced growth stocks and deeply discounted value stocks. Stocks in the middle 20% of U.S. market capitalization are defined as mid-cap. The blend style applies when neither growth nor value characteristics dominate.

U.S. Fund – Mid-Cap Growth: Mid-cap growth portfolios target U.S. companies projected to grow faster than other mid-cap stocks. These businesses tend to have higher prices relative to fundamentals and are often found in expanding industries. Stocks in the middle 20% of the U.S. equity market capitalization are considered mid-cap. Growth is defined by fast growth rates and high valuations.

U.S. Fund - Small Value: Small-value portfolios invest in small U.S. firms with valuations and growth rates below those of other small-cap peers. These companies tend to have lower price ratios, higher dividend yields, and slower growth rates. Stocks in the bottom 10% of U.S. equity market capitalization are defined as small cap. Value is defined by inexpensive valuations and slower growth fundamentals.

U.S. Fund - Small Blend: Small-blend portfolios favor U.S. firms at the smaller end of the market-capitalization range. Some aim to own an array of value and growth stocks while others employ a discipline that leads to holdings with valuations and growth rates close to the small-cap averages. Stocks in the bottom 10% of the capitalization of the U.S. equity market are defined as small cap. The blend style is assigned to portfolios where neither growth nor value characteristics predominate.

U.S. Fund - Small Growth: Small-growth portfolios focus on faster-growing U.S. companies in the lower end of the market-capitalization range. These portfolios typically emphasize younger firms or companies in dynamic industries. Because these companies grow quickly and tend to be richly valued, their stocks are generally more volatile. Stocks in the bottom 10% of U.S. market capitalization are considered small cap. Growth is defined by rapid growth and high valuations.
