data.title: NuScale Power: Revenue Generation Is Too Far Out
data.description:
The last time I wrote on NuScale Power (SMR), I gave the stock a hold after it had seen substantial price appreciation in prior months. The opposite is in effect now as the stock has tumbled, pulling back 55% since Halloween. There has been extreme volatility for the small modular nuclear reactor firm.

NuScale's potential nuclear reactor technology is based on its NuScale Power Module [NPM], which forms the core of their modular power plants known as VOYGR. Through a small, pressurized water reactor, their products can generate between 77 megawatts and 924 Mwe of total output.

Small nuclear reactors have some real upside as a more compact, carbon-free energy source that could provide better safety and steady power across several industries, especially in the data center realm. Unfortunately, the more I read and learn about the industry and SMR, the more I can see that commercialization for these reactors is still years away. SMR has taken on expenses that may never result in revenue, and it seems like that is just the beginning on the cost front, if contracts eventually do get put in place. The stock is hard to value based on current unprofitability, but seems to be trading at a premium, all while losses have ramped up. I rate SMR a sell.

By the Numbers

SMR reported Q3 adjusted EPS of -$1.85, missing expectations by $1.71. They generated $8.24 million in sales, which was 4x lower than Q4 last year. Although SMR is currently the only company with approval from the U.S. Nuclear Regulatory Commission [NRC], they still are only receiving sales from engineering services they provide to Ropower in Romania. With their technology yet to be commercialized, this is going to continue to be their only source of income and has no outlook to increase significantly. They mounted a net loss of $273.3 million in Q3 which is well above their recent loss trend.

Milestone Contribution 1

General and administrative expenses ramped up big time last quarter, due to the $495 million recognition of Milestone Contribution 1. This was their first scheduled payment obligation made to their strategic partner ENTRA1 Energy under their Partnership Milestones Agreement [PMA]. $148.5 million of this was paid in cash, with the remaining being recorded in accounts payable. This is a one-time payment, but it is one of three such payments as timelines progress. However, the additional $346.5 million liability will still hit their cash flows in future quarters as it is paid off.

The concern for me is that this expense has a chance to not even ever materialize, as there is still so much uncertainty with commercialization of the product. Milestone 1 was triggered by a non-binding agreement and will only receive sales when binding contracts occur. This is a huge expense taken on with hardly any sales being generated, and the forecast for possible large sales growth on commercialization is not expected until after 2030. If and when that does happen, additional expenses will remain as the plants operate, as well as the hefty second and third contribution payments. SMR is deep in the red, and the outlook to get in the green is likely 5-7 years away in the best-case scenario.

Contract Uncertainty

On a positive note, ENTRA1 has selected SMR for the deployment of up to 6 GW of power to the Tennessee Valley Authority [TVA] across seven state service regions. This is the biggest small modular reactor deployment the U.S. has ever seen. A clear positive step forward, but it is what triggered the Milestone 1 contribution leading to the massive quarterly loss. The downside is that while the agreement outlines intentions to collaborate on SMR deployment, it is non-binding and does not obligate TVA to purchase modules or ENTRA1 to sell them. Again, there is no revenue guaranteed from this, and even the good news SMR is receiving, is still not indicating for sure sales.

Fluor’s Exit and Downgrades

The biggest stakeholder in SMR at nearly 40% of the company, is Fluor Corp. (FLR). The company has published its monetization plan to fully exit its stake in SMR by Q2 2026.

They will convert their remaining Class B units into Class A shares and begin their process from there. Investors often interpret large shareholder exits as a signal of reduced confidence.

On top of the FLR exit, both Citi and UBS have downgraded their outlook on NuScale in the past few weeks, each slashing their price target by a significant 50%.

Earnings revisions are ugly for SMR with 6 downward revisions in the last 90 days and 0 upward revisions. The quant rating for SMR is at a yearly low as analysts from various sites and groups seem to be in agreement on the stock’s bleak outlook.

Valuation

The last time SMR had a price-to-book where it does now, the stock was trading near $12 per share. I think this indicates that a further pullback could still continue. SMR has a price-to-sales ratio of 62x, astronomically higher than the sector median. It will be important to keep an eye on the stock’s PB, as we will not see a PE ratio for the firm for likely at least five years or more.

Conclusion

I am changing my rating on SMR to a sell. I don’t necessarily think that the stock will fall much further, but I think there are better places to allocate your capital based on the discouraging outlook for greater revenue generation anytime soon. Small modular reactors are appealing with their ability to be installed in compact and remote locations. NuScale is a leader in this space with their approval from the NRC, but unless there is a drastic change in timeline on commercialization or prospective guaranteed contracts, I just don’t see enough here to warrant holding the stock. Revenue is going to be marginal for several years at least, and the current Milestone contribution will hurt cash flow for several quarters going into 2026. SMRs are an exciting power source on the horizon, but we are just way too far off.

data.title: Peloton: No Credit For The Turnaround
data.description:
If I were to give you two investment options below, it's pretty clear that Option B sounds more appealing.

Option A

~1.6 million subscribers

$1 billion of total revenue

Gross profit margin of ~39%

Adjusted EBITDA of $70M

Option B

~2.6 million subscribers

$2.4 billion to $2.5 billion of total revenue

Gross profit margin of ~52%

Adjusted EBITDA of $425M to $475M

Option A is Peloton's (PTON) FY 2021 guidance, and Option B is Peloton's FY 2026 guidance. Despite the business being much stronger now, at the time of the guidance announced in 2021, PTON's market cap was well north of $30 billion, while today it sits at less than one-tenth that—at $2.8 billion. It is currently trading at less than $7 per share and once traded at north of $150 per share (which was surely an unsustainable level in retrospect).

To be fair, the total addressable market was less known then, and growth was higher. However, I don't think the differential is close to what it should be, and I believe Peloton has not received the credit that it deserves for its turnaround. Further, Mr. Market is likely discounting Peloton precipitously because they are not considering several growth opportunities that I believe are apparent. I will first touch on a few key items from the latest quarter and then discuss the growth opportunities.

Latest Quarter

As I previously covered, Peloton's founder, John Foley, greatly overextended the company, which nearly led to its downfall. Since then, through two new CEOs, Peloton has slashed expenses and right-sized the business. Their SG&A expenses have fallen precipitously, which has allowed the business to return to profitability.

In the latest quarter, despite revenue coming in at $551 million (a 6% y/y decline), free cash flow was $67 million, an increase of $57 million y/y. By cutting unnecessary overhead and exiting many costly leases for spaces that were too large to justify, Peloton has been able to turn this business around. One questionable prior business decision under Foley was opening massive showrooms for just five products. At one point, Peloton had over 100 of these showrooms with costly leases. During the recent Morgan Stanley conference, current CEO Peter Stern remarked that they've been retiring those showrooms—with only single digits left—and replacing them with microstores that do about the same volume at one-tenth the size.

In addition to cutting overhead, Peloton was able to launch new products this quarter and released the Peloton Cross Training Series, its first full hardware portfolio refresh across its Bike, Tread, and Product lines. Importantly, Peloton finally launched a Pro Series, intended for commercial environments.

Key Growth Opportunities

Commercial

Peloton's initial growth story was led solely by consumers buying bikes for their homes. That was accelerated during the pandemic when demand went through the roof. Since then, folks have returned to gyms, and that fervent demand has cooled. There are only so many Americans that will buy a $2,000 bike. Peloton has expanded by focusing on selling the Tread and Rower, but again, the market in the U.S. for premium fitness products may be close to being saturated.

Given that saturation, Peloton is prudently focusing on growing its commercial business. Peloton had purchased Precor and had tried to sell it before but could not get the price it desired. In retrospect, holding on to Precor was likely a blessing in disguise because now Peloton can offer a more fulsome suite of products for commercial sales. Peloton launched a dedicated website for this side of its business called Peloton for Business. The focus is on selling to hotels, multifamily residential customers, corporations, and colleges. This represents a huge growth opportunity and one in which customers are less price sensitive. A dozen $2,000 bikes and rowers for a hotel or college is a drop in the bucket for those customers but could be the reason someone stays at the hotel or chooses the college.

One of Peloton's flagship customers for its college concept was the University of Texas. They've launched a dedicated space with bikes and rowers. Once a few colleges adopt this, it may lead to an influx of other colleges following suit to match the offering so as to not fall behind competitors.

International

In the latest quarter, of the $550 million in revenue generated, only $56 million was generated outside the U.S. The U.S. historically has been the primary focus for Peloton, but they are beginning to pivot and seek growth outside the U.S. This pivot was recently made possible with the addition of the auto-subtitles feature and AI dubbing. In the prior quarter's shareholder letter, this ambition was noted:

Internationally, we plan to deliver local, in-language experiences using a mix of native instruction, AI dubbing, and more flexible approaches to music for thousands of classes. Through partnerships, we aim to introduce the Peloton brand and experiences to millions of people around the world. Together, we believe these actions lay the groundwork for future, cost-effective launches of the full Peloton offering in new geographies.

Price-Sensitive Consumers

To date, Peloton's customers have been ones with discretionary income that are not price sensitive. This is part of the reason that their churn is so much lower than any other streaming company. However, as previously noted, this segment of the market is quite saturated, and Peloton must focus on selling to more price-sensitive consumers as well.

During their Black Friday sales, Peloton tested offering a competitively priced refurbished standard bike for just $695. During the Morgan Stanley conference, Peter Stern discussed this and noted that margins were still healthy for this offering and sales were strong, and they've kept this offer live on their website.

If you look at Amazon and search for exercise bikes, there are several that are priced between $150 and $800 that have several thousand reviews and recent sales. For example, one seemingly popular option on Amazon is a Schwinn bike priced at $800. There is clearly demand for a lower-priced bike. With Peloton offering a product with its premium features at or below that price, it can pick up customers that are more price sensitive and continue to grow its subscriber base.

Cross-Selling

The final vector of growth that I will touch on is cross-selling opportunities. Peloton just recently started to sell accessories for the bikes that were typically only sold through third parties, such as a fan, phone tray, and comfort saddle. These sales will be incremental revenue and profit but will not be game-changing. I think the game-changing opportunity is to grow into consumables via nutrition offerings.

You can say what you want about Peloton, but one thing that you can't question is the brand loyalty from its current subscribers. Peloton creates super fans who use the products, rave about them, wear the apparel, and follow their favorite instructors on social media. It's rare to find such loyalty in a product, and Peloton has the opportunity to capitalize on that. To date, they have not sold any consumables, i.e., products that customers will continue to use and purchase, such as nutrition offerings. Peloton has the opportunity to sell high-quality protein bars, powders, creatine, etc., and further integrate themselves into their subscribers's lives. They must be careful to sell only truly high-quality products so they don't dilute the brand, but the growth vector is apparent.

Valuation

Peloton is a unique business with no direct publicly traded peer group. The closest peer group that I've come up with includes Netflix Inc., Spotify Technology SA, Planet Fitness, Roku, Inc., and Match Group, Inc. In that group, I think Planet Fitness, Inc., is the closest peer. Their business model involves selling fitness subscriptions (both directly and indirectly through franchisees). They also sell equipment to their franchisees and operate certain locations. Planet Fitness recently guided for a 25% increase in their capex due to new corporate-owned locations, and moving forward, given Peloton's reduced footprint, I'd expect Peloton's relative capex spend to be lower than Planet Fitness'.

Since Peloton has just recently turned around its business, I think the fairest way to look at its relative trading range is to compare its enterprise value to revenue. If you look at the peer group, Peloton trades at about half the next lowest peer and at about 1/5th that of Planet Fitness'.

Another way to look at Peloton's valuation relative to its peer group is to look at its EV to EBITDA, which many hedge funds use to look at valuation given it is one of the cleanest ways to compare operating value across companies, especially in capital structure-sensitive or event-driven situations.

In this comparison, Peloton again falls at the lowest level in the peer group and at less than half of Planet Fitness'. If it were to trade at the same level as Planet Fitness, Peloton's stock would go up 2.5x and trade at about $17 per share. In my view, Planet Fitness is a much more mature business with more limited levers for growth.

If Peloton starts to execute on some new growth initiatives and the top line begins to expand, I think the market will agree with my sentiment. The market is forward-looking, so just small wins could lead to large valuation jumps. Given that it is difficult to forecast when that jump could occur. My one-year conservative price target is $15 per share, which, all things being equal, would result in Peloton still trading at the low end of the peer group on an EV to EBITDA basis and below that of Planet Fitness.

Risks

The biggest risk to my thesis is Peloton not being able to execute on any growth initiative and failing in every single one with revenue stagnation. To date, Peloton has not been able to effectively grow in any of the avenues I outlined above, so there can be no assurance that they will be successful in any of those endeavors.

If that is the case, and they cannot effectively grow the top line, I think the stock stays around the same valuation level. I have conviction in that call because if you look at Peloton's latest quarter, their subscription revenues accounted for the lion's share of their revenue ($400M of the $550M). Further, their subscriber churn is very low, and given the business had a large pull-forward effect during COVID, I think some of the subscribers they gained during that time were the most likely to churn and unsubscribe, and those have likely flocked away from the platform at this point.

Further, with every unsubscriber, that represents a re-engagement opportunity because that person is likely to sell their bike to someone else. Peloton can earn incremental revenue from that too because they recently launched a $95 activation fee for third-party sales. They also launched Repowered, a marketplace where they can receive 30% of the sale price (likely lower because their partner—Archive Resale Inc.—in the platform takes a cut of that 30%). So subscriber churn can represent a revenue growth opportunity (albeit a small one).

Conclusion

Peloton has faced a turbulent past but has finally found its footing and is cash flow positive. The market is pricing this like a cigar butt—a profitable but slowly declining business. However, I see it differently and see a once wounded company that has since healed and is poised to soar to new heights.
